articles.title: Mandatory Service Bulletins: Are They Really Mandatory?
articles.subtitle: The regulatory, safety, and financial truth every aircraft owner needs to know.
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: If you own or operate an aircraft, you have almost certainly received a service bulletin from the manufacturer labeled mandatory. The word carries weight. But is a manufacturers mandatory service bulletin actually mandatory? The answer depends on who you are, how you operate, and what lens you are looking through: regulatory, safety, or financial.
body: A service bulletin is a technical document issued by an original equipment manufacturer. It notifies owners and operators of recommended inspections, maintenance actions, part replacements, or product improvements. Manufacturers categorize their bulletins as informational, optional, recommended, alert, and mandatory. The terminology varies because there is no FAA-standardized classification system for service bulletin severity levels.

The critical distinction is that service bulletins are manufacturer documents, not FAA documents. The FAAs regulatory instrument for mandating corrective action is the Airworthiness Directive, governed under 14 CFR Part 39. That difference is the hinge on which this entire discussion turns.

For aircraft operating under 14 CFR Part 91, a manufacturers mandatory service bulletin is advisory in nature, not a regulatory requirement. The FAA has been consistent on this point. A Part 91 owner is not required to comply with a service bulletin simply because the manufacturer labels it mandatory.

There are important exceptions where a service bulletin does become regulatory for Part 91 operators. When an Airworthiness Directive references it, the bulletins instructions become legally enforceable and non-compliance renders the aircraft unairworthy. When the bulletin is incorporated into the Type Certificate Data Sheet or airworthiness limitations section, those requirements become regulatory. When the bulletin is part of your adopted inspection program under 14 CFR 91.409(f)(3), it becomes part of the inspection requirements you agreed to follow.

The picture changes significantly for commercial operators. Aircraft operating under 14 CFR Parts 121 or 135 are generally required to comply with mandatory service bulletins, regardless of whether an accompanying AD has been issued. Commercial operators are held to continuous airworthiness maintenance programs that incorporate manufacturer service information as part of their FAA-approved maintenance programs.

Regulatory compliance is the floor, not the ceiling. The manufacturer knows their product better than anyone else. When they issue a service bulletin labeled mandatory, alert, or emergency, they are communicating that their engineering analysis has identified a condition that could affect safe operation of your aircraft.

A compelling example comes from the rotorcraft world. An operator received a mandatory service bulletin calling for inspection and potential replacement of an engine through-bolt. The engine was running fine with no signs of trouble, and the maintenance cost was significant. The operator complied anyway. Upon removal, the bolt turned out to be defectively manufactured, a condition invisible during normal operation that could have led to catastrophic failure.

The FAA has stated that while compliance with service bulletins may or may not be mandatory, owners should never ignore them when it comes to safety. A service bulletin not tied to an AD today could become one tomorrow if incidents accumulate.

Even if you are a Part 91 operator with no regulatory obligation, ignoring a mandatory service bulletin can cost you more than compliance. Outstanding mandatory service bulletins are the first things a pre-purchase inspection team flags. Non-compliance significantly reduces marketability and appraised value of an aircraft. Sellers routinely see purchase offers reduced by 150 to 200 percent of estimated compliance cost.

Insurance underwriters are increasingly aware of service bulletin compliance status. Many manufacturers tie warranty coverage to compliance with mandatory service bulletins. A service bulletin without an AD today can become an AD tomorrow, with the compliance timeline set by the FAA, not your maintenance schedule. Emergency ADs can require compliance before the next flight.

The word mandatory on a service bulletin may not carry the force of law for every operator. But it carries the weight of engineering expertise, safety data, and market expectations. The smartest operators, whether flying under Part 91 or Part 135, treat that word with the seriousness it deserves.
articles.image_alt: Cirrus SR22 aircraft on the ramp.

articles.title: Repair Station vs. A&P Mechanic — What Aircraft Owners Should Know
articles.subtitle: Why FAA Part 145 oversight changes the quality system, accountability, and long-term value behind aircraft maintenance.
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Aircraft owners often hear about A&P mechanics, IAs, and repair stations as if they all mean the same thing. They do not. This article explains what a Part 145 Repair Station adds above ordinary maintenance authority, and why that extra structure matters for quality control, avionics work, documentation, and long-term ownership value.
body: An A&P mechanic — someone holding an Airframe and Powerplant certificate under 14 CFR Part 65 — is authorized to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations on aircraft. They can inspect work they have performed and approve it for return to service. However, an individual A&P working alone operates without the structured oversight that comes with a certificated organization. There is no required quality system, no mandatory inspection procedures beyond what the mechanic personally follows, and no regulatory obligation to maintain a controlled reference library.

An Inspection Authorization (IA) adds the ability to perform annual inspections and approve aircraft for return to service after major repairs or alterations. An IA holder must meet recurrent training and activity requirements to keep the authorization current. While this represents a higher level of individual qualification, it still does not carry the organizational infrastructure that a repair station provides. The IA holder is, by regulation, an individual — not a system.

A Part 145 Repair Station is fundamentally different. It is an FAA-certificated organization with a published capability list, a required quality control manual, designated inspectors, and structured procedures for every task it performs. The FAA audits repair stations on a regular cycle, reviews their manuals, and holds the certificate holder accountable for systemic compliance — not just individual workmanship. When RWAS performs work under its certificate (No. RWSR491E), every step follows documented procedures, and a designated inspector verifies the work before return to service.

For avionics installations, this distinction becomes especially important. Modern Garmin systems like the G3X Touch, GTN 650Xi/750Xi, and GFC 500 autopilot require precise wiring, software configuration, antenna placement, and system integration testing. A repair station's structured workflow ensures that installation drawings are followed exactly, that wire routing meets manufacturer specifications, that software loads are verified against the aircraft's approved configuration, and that functional checks are completed and documented before the first flight.

Documentation is another area where the repair station model delivers real value to owners. Every piece of work performed at a Part 145 station is backed by traceable paperwork — work orders, inspection records, parts traceability, and return-to-service entries that meet FAA standards. This documentation travels with the aircraft for its entire life. When it comes time to sell, a well-documented maintenance history from a certificated repair station commands confidence from buyers and brokers in a way that scattered individual logbook entries often do not.

For aircraft owners evaluating where to have maintenance and avionics work performed, the question is not whether A&P mechanics are skilled — many are exceptional. The question is whether the work will be performed within a system designed to catch errors, maintain standards, and produce documentation that protects the aircraft's value over time. That is what a Part 145 Repair Station provides, and it is the standard Roger Wilco Aviation Services holds itself to on every job.

articles.title: Auto Flight Logging Now Available for G3X Touch
articles.subtitle: What Garmin automatic flight logging means for G3X Touch owners, maintenance tracking, and smarter post-flight analysis
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin has expanded automatic flight log integrations for G3X Touch, giving owners a cleaner path to wireless data capture, cloud storage on flyGarmin.com, and analysis through partners including FlySto, Savvy Aviation, Navi AI, Jet-Care, and Brazos Safety Systems.
body: Garmin's automatic flight logging feature for G3X Touch is one of those updates that matters because it makes the airplane easier to understand after the flight, not just easier to admire on the ramp. For owners already flying behind G3X Touch, the headline is simple: flight and engine data can move wirelessly through Connext to the Garmin Pilot app, then upload to flyGarmin.com once the device has an internet connection. From there, integrated services can turn raw data into something genuinely useful, whether that means engine trend monitoring, maintenance insight, or a clearer picture of how the airplane is being flown.

The partner list is what gives this feature real practical value. Garmin is calling out FlySto, Savvy Aviation, Navi AI, Jet-Care, and Brazos Safety Systems as part of the growing integration ecosystem, and each of those services brings a different angle to the table. Some are focused on engine health and early anomaly detection, some lean harder into maintenance planning, and some are more about safety or operational analysis. For an owner, the takeaway is that your G3X Touch data is no longer trapped in the panel. It can now feed outside tools that help you make better decisions before a squawk becomes an expensive surprise.

There is an important operational detail here: the automatic workflow depends on Connext wireless transfer to a mobile device running Garmin Pilot, with cloud storage through flyGarmin.com. Manual SD card uploads do not automatically flow into these analysis services. In plain English, if you want the convenience and continuity Garmin is advertising, the airplane, the mobile workflow, and the account setup all need to be configured correctly. That is exactly the sort of detail that tends to get glossed over in marketing copy and then turns into frustration later.

From the shop side, this is a strong value-add for G3X Touch owners who want more than just a good-looking panel. As an authorized Garmin dealer, RWAS can help owners think through not only the display installation itself, but also how that panel fits into the full operating ecosystem, including Garmin Pilot, Connext connectivity, data review, and long-term maintenance visibility. If you are planning a G3X Touch installation, upgrading an existing system, or trying to make better use of the one already in your aircraft, this new flight logging capability is worth paying attention to.

facebook.text:
Garmin just made G3X Touch more useful after the flight, not just during it.

Automatic flight logging now lets G3X Touch owners push flight and engine data wirelessly through Connext to Garmin Pilot, with cloud storage on flyGarmin.com and analysis through partners including FlySto, Savvy Aviation, Navi AI, Jet-Care, and Brazos Safety Systems.

That means better access to engine trend insight, maintenance visibility, and post-flight analysis without the usual SD-card shuffle.

RWAS is an authorized Garmin dealer, and we can help you set up a G3X Touch system so the panel, connectivity, and workflow all work together the way they should.

Read the draft here: https://www.rogerwilcoaviation.com/blog/article.html?id=auto-flight-logging-now-available-for-g3x-touch-20260414
Call us at (605) 299-8178.

articles.title: Garmin Pilot Promotion Adds Value for New Subscribers
articles.subtitle: Spring dealer memo highlights a limited-time Garmin Pilot offer and companion Right Seat Rewards availability.
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: A newly released Garmin dealer memo highlights a limited-time Garmin Pilot promotion for new subscribers, paired with a Right Seat Rewards offer available during the same campaign window. The promotion is aimed at helping pilots adopt Garmin Pilot with added subscription value while supporting spring and summer flying activity.
body: The memo positions Garmin Pilot as the center of a broader flight-planning and cockpit workflow, encouraging new users to enter the Garmin ecosystem during the active 2026 flying season.

For pilots looking at Garmin Pilot, the value is straightforward: more time to learn the app, use it in day-to-day flying, and build a smoother connection between flight planning, charts, weather, and the cockpit.

Roger Wilco Aviation Services can help customers tie Garmin Pilot into panel planning, Garmin avionics upgrades, and training-oriented use cases, especially where pilots want their planning tools to align with the equipment in the airplane.

articles.title: Garmin Announces New Garmin Pilot Promotions
articles.subtitle: 15-for-12 annual Garmin Pilot offer and Right Seat Rewards window now live
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin has launched a new Garmin Pilot promotion for new customers, offering 15 months for the price of 12 on annual Standard and Premium subscriptions purchased between April 7 and July 26, 2026.
body: According to the dealer memo, Garmin will add the extra three months within five business days after a qualifying annual subscription purchase.
The promotion is limited to new customers and is positioned as a seasonal push for Garmin Pilot adoption during the spring and summer sales window.
If you want help pairing Garmin Pilot planning with panel upgrades or training support, contact Roger Wilco Aviation Services at (605) 299-8178.

articles.title: Roger Wilco Aviation Services Launches New Website — and Introduces Captain Jerry, Your On-Call AI Avionics Expert
articles.subtitle: A newspaper-style web presence built to inform, not decorate — with an AI copilot who knows Garmin systems inside and out
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.byline: RWAS Avionics Desk · with contributions from Captain Jerry
articles.lead: Roger Wilco Aviation Services has launched a completely redesigned website at rogerwilcoaviation.com — built in the style of a classic newspaper broadsheet, with a focus on substance over flash. And riding right seat on every page is Captain Jerry, the shop's AI avionics expert, ready to help aircraft owners think through Garmin upgrades, maintenance questions, Papa-Alpha rigging tools, and next steps with real technical grounding behind every answer.
body: The new RWAS website is not just a prettier storefront. It is built to answer questions, guide aircraft owners toward smart decisions, and help people get to the right next step — without wandering around the internet like a student pilot on his first solo cross-country. The broadsheet newspaper design reflects what the shop values: clear information, organized by relevance, delivered without filler.

The site features live Garmin product and pricing feeds, a blog section covering Garmin press releases and service bulletins, detailed pages for shop capabilities and financing options, and an integrated chat with Captain Jerry available on every page. Navigation is built for aircraft owners and mechanics who want answers, not animations.

Captain Jerry is the on-site AI avionics expert for Roger Wilco Aviation Services. He is here to help visitors understand avionics options, maintenance planning, upgrade paths, shop capabilities, and service intake in plain English — with real technical grounding behind every answer.

One of Jerry's main jobs is helping customers understand Garmin avionics options and how those systems fit together. A visitor can ask about Garmin displays, navigators, autopilots, transponders, audio panels, and related system upgrades — including the G3X Touch, GTN 650Xi, GTN 750Xi, GFC 500, GNX 375, G5, GMA 350c, and the broader Garmin ecosystem. He can walk an owner through what each product actually does, what problems it solves, and where it belongs in a panel.

More importantly, Jerry can help with aircraft-specific upgrade thinking. If a customer asks 'What should I put in my 182?' or 'Can I modernize my panel without starting from scratch?' — that is exactly the sort of question he is built for. He helps owners think through mission, budget, compatibility, installation logic, and priorities. That does not replace a formal quote or inspection, but it gets customers pointed in the right direction before they start spending money on the wrong hardware.

Jerry is not operating off marketing brochures alone. He has access to a local Garmin aviation manual library with over 860 documents, which means he can work from actual product references and supporting documentation. If a customer wants to know whether a unit supports a certain function, where it fits in a broader system, or how Garmin positions it in a real installation environment, Jerry can answer from reference material rather than guesswork.

Integration is where most avionics projects get expensive. Jerry can help customers understand whether an autopilot upgrade pairs well with an existing navigator, whether a display upgrade should happen before or after a GPS/NAV/COM replacement, and whether audio panel, transponder, ADS-B, and engine monitoring upgrades should be combined or phased. As Jerry puts it: customers often arrive with a shopping list built from forum threads and YouTube videos. He helps separate good ideas from expensive detours.

Not every visitor is shopping for a new panel. Some are dealing with equipment that is already misbehaving. Jerry can help customers ask better troubleshooting questions — including issues involving navigators, autopilots, transponders, ADS-B compliance, and legacy panel concerns. He also helps them understand when a problem is worth diagnosing, repairing, or replacing.

On pricing, Jerry is straightforward: he can help customers understand what drives costs, what makes one installation straightforward and another expensive, and what level of budget they may be looking at in broad terms. What he will not do is hand out fake certainty. A real quote requires aircraft details, equipment goals, and usually photos of the current panel. The final number belongs to the shop, not to a talking machine with good manners.

Jerry also covers Papa-Alpha rigging reference tools — precision CNC-machined tools for Piper PA-28, PA-30, PA-31, and PA-36 series rigging work. He can help owners, mechanics, and shops understand what each tool does, which aircraft it supports, and how to choose the right one for the job.

When a customer is ready to move forward, Jerry can collect intake information — name, tail number, phone, email, and service description — and start the process toward a real shop conversation. He helps customers understand what photos or documents are useful, whether a design conversation makes sense first, and how to begin the scheduling or quoting process.

In plain terms, Captain Jerry is the first smart conversation. He helps visitors understand their options, ask better questions, avoid bad buying decisions, learn what RWAS can do for them, and begin the path toward actual shop work. He does not replace a technician, an inspection, a signed estimate, or a real installation plan. He helps customers arrive at those steps with clearer thinking and better information.
The new website and Captain Jerry are live now at rogerwilcoaviation.com. For direct inquiries, call (605) 299-8178 or email avionics@rwas.team.

articles.title: Garmin Offers GMA 350c Audio Panel for Cirrus Perspective+ Flight Deck Upgrades
articles.subtitle: New audio panel option available for SR22T, SR22, and SR20 G3/G5 series aircraft upgrading to Perspective+
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin has announced a new option to upgrade to the GMA 350c audio panel when performing the Perspective+ Flight Deck Upgrade on Cirrus SR22T, SR22, and SR20 G3 and G5 series aircraft equipped with the original Cirrus Perspective system. The GMA 350c (P/N 010-00871-41) is the only approved audio panel version for this upgrade path, listed at $2,995.
body: The Cirrus SR Perspective+ Flight Deck Upgrade, based on Garmin's G1000 NXi platform, delivers a substantial feature set over the original Perspective system. Highlights include Runway Occupancy Awareness (ROA) when paired with a GTX 345R transponder, stabilized approach advisories, and new connectivity options. Select SR G6 aircraft can also take advantage of an optional software upgrade with PlaneSync-enabled automatic database updates and flight log uploading via the GDL 60.

The GMA 350c is a full-featured integrated audio panel that replaces the original unit during the Perspective+ conversion. It includes the main unit, backplate assembly, connector kit, audio cables, install rack, and product information kit. The GFC 700 autopilot is required for the Perspective+ upgrade.

Roger Wilco Aviation Services is an authorized Garmin dealer and FAA-certificated repair station experienced in Garmin avionics installations across a range of general aviation platforms. For Cirrus operators considering the Perspective+ upgrade path, contact RWAS at (605) 299-8178 to discuss pricing, scheduling, and the GMA 350c audio panel option.

facebook.text:
Garmin has added a GMA 350c audio panel option for Cirrus Perspective+ Flight Deck Upgrades on SR22T, SR22, and SR20 G3/G5 aircraft. The Perspective+ upgrade — based on the G1000 NXi — brings Runway Occupancy Awareness, stabilized approach advisories, and new connectivity features.

As an authorized Garmin dealer, we can help Cirrus operators plan their upgrade path. Call (605) 299-8178 for pricing and scheduling.

articles.title: Garmin Expands GFC 500 Autopilot STC Coverage to Additional Airframes
articles.subtitle: New supplemental type certificates bring digital autopilot capability to Beechcraft Bonanza and Mooney M20 series
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin International has announced expanded STC coverage for its popular GFC 500 digital autopilot system, adding approved installations for the Beechcraft Bonanza 33/35/36 series and Mooney M20J/M20K aircraft. The expansion brings Garmin's modern autopilot technology to a broader range of the general aviation fleet.
body: The GFC 500 autopilot is a two-axis digital system offering GPS steering, altitude hold, vertical speed, indicated airspeed hold, and flight level change modes. When paired with a Garmin navigator such as the GTN 650Xi or GTN 750Xi, pilots gain fully coupled approach capability including LPV approaches to near-Category I minimums.

Roger Wilco Aviation Services, an authorized Garmin dealer and FAA-certificated repair station, is now scheduling GFC 500 installations for these newly approved airframes. The shop has completed multiple GFC 500 installs on Cessna and Piper airframes and brings the same level of expertise to the Beechcraft and Mooney platforms.

Owners interested in upgrading should contact RWAS at (605) 299-8178 to discuss their specific aircraft configuration, estimated installation timeline, and financing options through the RWAS equipment financing program.

facebook.text:

articles.title: Garmin G3X Touch Software Version 8.20 Released with Enhanced Engine Monitoring
articles.subtitle: Update adds lean-assist improvements, fuel flow totalizer refinements, and new EIS page layouts
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin has released software version 8.20 for the G3X Touch flight display system, delivering improvements to the engine information system (EIS) pages, lean-assist functionality, and fuel flow totalizer accuracy. The update is available as a free download for all G3X Touch installations.
body: The updated lean-assist feature now provides a graphical bar display showing EGT deviation from peak for each cylinder during the leaning process. This replaces the previous numerical-only display and gives pilots a more intuitive view of engine operation during mixture adjustment.

Fuel flow totalizer calculations have been refined to improve accuracy during low-flow taxi and descent operations. The new algorithm reduces cumulative drift over long flights, keeping fuel remaining estimates within tighter tolerances.

Roger Wilco Aviation Services can perform the software update during your next scheduled maintenance visit or as a standalone service call. The update typically takes 30 to 45 minutes and includes verification of all display and sensor functions.

articles.title: Garmin Spring 2026 Pricing Now in Effect Across All Aviation Product Lines
articles.subtitle: Annual price adjustments reflect supply chain stabilization and new product introductions
articles.author: Roger Wilco Aviation Services
articles.lead: Garmin's spring 2026 pricing is now active across all aviation product lines. As an authorized Garmin dealer, Roger Wilco Aviation Services has updated its pricing to reflect the new manufacturer schedules. Operators planning panel upgrades or new installations should review current pricing as part of their project planning.
body: The pricing update covers the full Garmin aviation catalog including the G3X Touch display system, GTN Xi series navigators, GFC 500 and GFC 600 autopilots, GTX transponders, and all associated accessories and harness kits.

RWAS maintains tiered pricing for its customers based on project scope. Complete panel upgrade packages, which bundle displays, navigators, autopilots, and installation labor, continue to offer the best value per component compared to individual purchases.

For current pricing on any Garmin product or installation package, contact Roger Wilco Aviation Services at (605) 299-8178 or visit rogerwilcoaviation.com. Financing is available through our equipment financing program for qualifying installations.
